title: Records and immutable data

canonicalExplanation.summary: A Java record declares a transparent nominal carrier whose components define generated accessors, construction, equality, hashing, and representation, while validation can live in a compact constructor.

sections.heading: Start here
sections.body: Start with this case: A Point record validates finite coordinates and relies on generated component accessors and value equality. Your goal is to choose and implement a record or class for a stated domain model and justify the choice. Work through the example one step at a time, then use the boundary to check your answer.

sections.heading: Before you begin
sections.body: Review these declared prerequisites first: Method contracts and parameters; pf data records; References, identity and equality. If one is unfamiliar, revisit it before continuing.

sections.heading: Language-specific operational model
sections.body: A Java record declares a transparent nominal carrier whose components define generated accessors, construction, equality, hashing, and representation, while validation can live in a compact constructor. This model is applied through the declared Java: Beginner to Intermediate behavior rather than a language-neutral slogan.

sections.heading: Boundary and failure model
sections.body: A record is shallowly immutable, component references may denote mutable objects, records cannot extend another class, and hidden mutable representation or identity-rich lifecycle may call for a class. The learner must reject this documented misconception: Record components and every object reachable from them become deeply immutable automatically.

includes: Use records for transparent immutable carriers and classes when behavior or hidden representation dominates.
Choose and implement a record or class for a stated domain model and justify the choice.
excludes: A record is shallowly immutable, component references may denote mutable objects, records cannot extend another class, and hidden mutable representation or identity-rich lifecycle may call for a class.

outcomes: Choose and implement a record or class for a stated domain model and justify the choice.

examples.title: Records and immutable data: direct language case
examples.situation: A Point record validates finite coordinates and relies on generated component accessors and value equality.
walkthrough: Step 1 — Observe the concrete Java: Beginner to Intermediate state or tool result: A Point record validates finite coordinates and relies on generated component accessors and value equality.
Step 2 — Use the core rule above to predict one observable result.
Step 3 — Verify the result with this correction: Use a record for transparent shallowly immutable data and defensively copy mutable components when deep isolation is part of the contract.
examples.result: The example meets the atomic outcome because Use a record for transparent shallowly immutable data and defensively copy mutable components when deep isolation is part of the contract.

examples.title: Records and immutable data: boundary and transfer case
examples.situation: A domain aggregate with hidden cache and state transitions remains a class because transparent component state is not its intended public contract.
walkthrough: Step 1 — Name what changed from the direct case: A domain aggregate with hidden cache and state transitions remains a class because transparent component state is not its intended public contract.

Step 2 — Keep the language boundary explicit: A record is shallowly immutable, component references may denote mutable objects, records cannot extend another class, and hidden mutable representation or identity-rich lifecycle may call for a class.
Step 3 — Reject the tempting but incorrect rule: Record components and every object reachable from them become deeply immutable automatically.
examples.result: This case demonstrates the declared outcome: Choose and implement a record or class for a stated domain model and justify the choice.

misconceptions.mistakenBelief: Record components and every object reachable from them become deeply immutable automatically.
misconceptions.correction: Use a record for transparent shallowly immutable data and defensively copy mutable components when deep isolation is part of the contract.
misconceptions.diagnosticPrompt: Predict both examples and identify the first observable contradiction in this belief: Record components and every object reachable from them become deeply immutable automatically.

analogy.example: a secured service object whose constructor admits only valid state and whose public desk exposes approved behavior can introduce Records and immutable data, after the canonical Java: Beginner to Intermediate rule is stated first.
usefulFor: understanding encapsulation, construction, identity, equality, and records
limitations: object references, hashing, shallow immutability, and garbage collection are not physical ownership rules
